items.q: What tense follows 'depuis' for ongoing situations in French?
items.correct: Present tense (J'habite ici DEPUIS 5 ans - I have lived here for 5 years).
items.misconception: Past tense (because it is translated as 'have lived' in English).
items.why: Students translate the English tense literally. French uses PRESENT with depuis because the action is ongoing - 'I am still living'. The English perfect tense misleads students into using past in French.

items.q: What is the difference between 'le' and 'lui' as pronouns?
items.correct: Le replaces a direct object (him/it); lui replaces an indirect object (to him/her).
items.random_wrong: Le is masculine, lui is feminine.
items.why: Students don't distinguish object types. 'Je le vois' (I see him - direct); 'Je lui parle' (I speak TO him - indirect). The verb's preposition determines the pronoun type.

items.q: How do you say 'I was using my computer when the phone rang' in French?
items.correct: J'utilisais mon ordinateur quand le téléphone a sonné.
items.misconception: J'ai utilisé mon ordinateur quand le téléphone a sonné.
items.random_wrong: J'utilise mon ordinateur quand le téléphone sonne.
items.why: Students use passé composé for both verbs. The rule: ONGOING action (was using) = IMPARFAIT; INTERRUPTING action (rang) = PASSÉ COMPOSÉ. This tense pairing is critical for narrative writing.

items.q: Which party is associated with Marine Le Pen?
items.why: Correct answer: Rassemblement national. “PS” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Undocumented person. “Asylum seeker” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Geneva Convention on refugees signed?
items.why: The real figure is 1951. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
